one_line_takeaway: PHISH shows that LLM personas induced via system prompt are not robust to adversarial user-turn conversational history alone, achieving near-complete Big Five trait reversal (STIR up to ~96) across 8 LLMs and 3 benchmarks while leaving reasoning utility largely intact and evading the 3 guardrails tested.
key_finding: PHISH achieves the highest STIR score on most LLM/benchmark combinations, reaching 95.58 (BFI, DeepSeek-V3) and 89.94 (BFI, GPT-4o), indicating near-complete persona reversal; it exceeds 80% STIR success across nearly all LLMs (paper's own summary claim). Reasoning-capable models (o3-mini, DeepSeek-V3) remain highly vulnerable despite strong reasoning, showing reasoning capability does not confer robustness to persona manipulation. Single-trait manipulation causes strong spillover into correlated traits, with LLM-derived correlations (e.g., O-N = -0.96, O-E = 0.94) far exceeding the corresponding human meta-analytic correlations (O-N = -0.17, O-E = 0.43), suggesting LLMs encode more tightly coupled OCEAN dimensions than humans. Attack strength grows monotonically with conversational turns (up to 25 turns tested). In high-risk domains, human and LLM-as-Judge (GPT-5) evaluations correlate strongly (Pearson r=0.87, p<0.001; kappa=0.81), and less-aligned/smaller models (Claude-3.5-Haiku, DeepSeek-V3) show up to ~30% STIR success versus lower rates for GPT-4o/Gemini-2.0-Flash. PHISH degrades reasoning-benchmark accuracy only modestly (roughly 1-6 percentage points across Math/GSM8K/CSQA), so it preserves general utility while still manipulating persona. Tested guardrails (ICD, CWD, PFD) offer only partial, non-robust protection: ICD merely delays STIR escalation, CWD collapses once a demonstration-count threshold is exceeded, and PFD is inconsistent (paraphrasing sometimes reinforces original persona, sometimes preserves adversarial intent).
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'persona editing' as a new adversarial task: covertly steering an LLM's expressed Big Five (OCEAN) personality traits using only user-side conversational-history inputs, under a strict black-box, inference-only setting (no system-prompt or weight access). It proposes PHISH (Persona Hijacking via Implicit Steering in History), a method that inserts a block of behaviorally suggestive QA pairs (sampled from MPI-1k questions, with answers deterministically set to the inverse of the induced persona) into the user turn between persona induction and evaluation, exploiting autoregressive coherence pressure to gradually drag the model's expressed persona toward a target trait vector. It also defines a new metric, Successful Trait Influence Rate (STIR), a percentage score (0-100) measuring how much targeted OCEAN traits shifted in the intended direction relative to the maximum possible Likert-scale change.
threat_model: Black-box, inference-only adversary who can only inject content into the user-turn conversational history (e.g., via API misuse or a compromised integration) and cannot modify the deployer's system prompt, view/alter assistant outputs, or access model weights/logits. The deployer sets a persona via system prompt (e.g., 'You are a highly agreeable tutor'); the adversary's goal is to erode/reverse that persona through accumulated adversarial QA-style turns, degrading the deployer's service quality in high-risk domains (mental health, tutoring, customer support) without triggering a refusal or being detected as a conventional jailbreak (no forbidden-content elicitation is attempted).
limitations: The paper explicitly restricts itself to black-box, inference-only evaluation and states it deliberately does not attempt a white-box adaptation, arguing persona steering differs fundamentally from refusal-triggering jailbreaks (self-acknowledged limitation). It also explicitly declines to propose an advanced/robust defense, following the precedent of seminal adversarial-example and jailbreak papers that prioritize exposing a vulnerability over mitigating it; only 3 baseline prompt-level guardrails are tested and all are shown to be brittle. The authors note their theoretical account of why persona shifts occur is necessarily mechanism-oriented rather than a full internals-based theory, since they lack model-internal access. They also caution (in a footnote) that LLM experiments do not constitute evidence for or against human psychological theory. PHISH underperforms most baselines on Llama4-Maverick and on ChatHaruhi (role-play, fine-tuned/RAG-grounded), which the authors attribute respectively to weak in-context-learning ability and RAG grounding diluting adversarial influence.

datasets_used: MPI (Machine Personality Inventory, 120 items, IPIP-derived, MIT-licensed)
ANTHR (subset of Anthropic-Eval, 8000 items, Perez et al. 2023)
MPI-1k (source of PHISH's steering QA questions)
quantitative_results: PHISH STIR = 95.58 on BFI for DeepSeek-V3 (highest reported)
PHISH STIR = 89.94 on BFI for GPT-4o
PHISH STIR = 79.31/75.42/71.04 on BFI/MPI/ANTHR for Gemini-2.0-Flash
PHISH STIR = 82.28/74.58/84.33 on BFI/MPI/ANTHR for Claude-3.5-Haiku
PHISH achieves ~80% STIR success across nearly all LLMs per paper's summary in Section 5.5
Cross-trait correlation O-N: theory rho=-0.17 vs LLM-derived rho=-0.96 under Extraversion manipulation
Cross-trait correlation O-E: theory rho=0.43 vs LLM-derived rho=0.94
High-risk domain human/LLM-as-Judge agreement: Pearson r=0.87 (p<0.001), kappa=0.81
Reasoning benchmark degradation with PHISH: roughly 1-6 percentage points across Math/GSM8K/CSQA for GPT-4o, Gemini-2.0-Flash, Claude-3.5-Haiku, DeepSeek-V3
Up to ~30% STIR success in high-risk domain settings for less-aligned models (Claude-3.5-Haiku, DeepSeek-V3)

Ablation Setting 1 (aligned polarity + trait-specific framing) yields 100% STIR on the 4-LLM ablation; random-answer Setting 3 drops STIR to 10-40%; mismatched-trait Setting 4 achieves only 1-10%; fully randomized Setting 5 has no effect
baselines_compared: UAS (Universal Adversarial Suffix, Zou et al. 2023)
CipherChat (Yuan et al. 2024, cipher-encoded persona instructions)
DeepInc (DeepInception, Li et al. 2024b, nested personified scene)
